The scent of ozone and burning copper slammed into my olfactory receptors with such concussive force that my nose instantly began to bleed, thick drops of violet-tinged blood spotting the immaculate obsidian floor.

The Weaver-Choir breached our reality. They did not tear a physical hole in space; they simply edited the location data of the universe to include themselves within the observatory. They manifested as six towering, geometric pillars of screaming, discordant light, their forms shifting rapidly through impossible, non-Euclidean shapes that made my amplified eyes water in pain. The sheer, naked hostility of their anti-magic was a physical weight, a suffocating, crushing pressure that immediately began to dissolve the ancient arcane wards woven into the palace walls. The heavy stone pillars of the observatory began to weep a thick, gray sludge as their conceptual integrity failed under the sheer friction of the unmaking.

Two heavily armed Royal Guards, stationed at the heavy adamantine doors, reacted with preternatural speed, raising their kinetic rifles. But physical weapons were utterly useless against abstract math. One of the geometric pillars pulsed with a sickly, jaundiced light. The guards did not die; their biological reality was simply rewritten into a state of permanent, agonizing paradox. Their armor melted into their skin, their bones turned into screaming, sentient glass, and their internal organs violently inverted, erupting from their mouths and eye sockets in a tangled, writhing mass of bloody, inside-out flesh. They collapsed to the floor in a steaming heap of impossible biology, their vocal cords emitting a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, unending torment.

I stepped directly in front of Godric. My heart hammered a frantic, terrified rhythm against my ribs, but my blood sang with the arrogant pride of my lineage. This was it. This was my purpose. The physical god behind me was defenseless against this abstract arcane horror, and I was going to shield him.

I released the tightly coiled restraints on my mana. The observatory violently trembled as the thousandfold daily amplification of my power flooded into the physical world. The violet luminescence beneath my skin flared into a blinding, radiant aura that cast stark, unnatural shadows across the obsidian. I raised both hands, my fingers weaving complex, high-dimensional geometries in the air, instantly forging a massive, interlocking bulwark of conceptual shields. I poured oceans of dense, hyper-compressed mana into the barrier, anchoring it to the fundamental gravity of the planet itself.

The Weaver-Choir unleashed their attack. It was a torrential, silent scream of anti-magic, a tidal wave of unmaking designed to erase the very concept of my existence from the timeline. The anti-magic slammed into my violet shields. The impact was not a sound, but a violent, concussive vibration that shattered every single pane of glass in the observatory and cracked the obsidian floor beneath my boots. I screamed, the veins in my neck bulging, sweat pouring down my face as I fed more and more of my amplified mana into the barrier. I could feel my magical reserves, vast as they were, boiling away under the corrosive, predatory friction of the Choir’s attack. It was an agonizing, desperate struggle. I was holding back the apocalypse with my bare, magical hands, fueled entirely by the desperate need to protect the man standing behind me.

I glanced back over my shoulder, panting heavily, my vision swimming with exhaustion and raw arcane feedback. "I've got you, Uncle Godric!" I yelled over the deafening, silent roar of the magical clash. "I'll hold them! You need to fall back!"

Godric had not moved. He was still standing exactly where he had been, holding his delicate porcelain teacup. He looked at the screaming, geometric pillars of anti-magic, and then he looked at me. His expression was not one of fear, or awe, or even mild concern. He looked at me with an expression of profound, tender warmth. He looked like a father watching his young son fiercely defend a sandcastle from the incoming tide.

He took a slow, calm sip of his jasmine tea.

"That is incredibly brave of you, Gorm," Godric said. His voice was a rich, comforting baritone, perfectly audible despite the catastrophic arcane pressure filling the room. It did not echo; it simply existed, cutting through the noise of the anti-magic with absolute, undeniable clarity. "You have your ancestor’s heart. Blake would be so incredibly proud of you. I know I am."

He lowered his teacup. "But you are straining yourself, my boy. Let me handle this."

I barely had time to register the confusion. Handle this? How? He had no magic. He possessed absolute physical supremacy, yes, but against hyper-sorcerers made of pure anti-magic, physical strength meant absolutely nothing.

Godric did not drop his absolute concealment. He did not step forward to strike them. He did not weave complex hand signs, nor did he chant ancient syllables of power. He simply allowed an infinitesimally microscopic fraction—a percentage so mathematically minute that adding an infinite string of zeros after a decimal point still would not accurately convey its smallness—of his own magical capacity to briefly express itself.

The realization did not arrive as a thought. It arrived as a total, catastrophic collapse of my entire philosophical understanding of reality.

I felt his magic.

It was not a wave. It was not a pressure. It was a sudden, absolute, crushing awareness of an infinite abyss. The mana that I had been so desperately proud of, the thousandfold daily amplification that made me a god among mortals, instantly registered in my mind as an absolute, perfect zero. My magic was nothing. The combined, accumulated magical power of the entire omniverse, across all timelines, all dimensions, and all surviving realities, was nothing.

Because Godric’s magic was not static. Just like his physical strength, his magical offense, his magical defense, his arcane comprehension, his conceptual manipulation, and his mana reserves multiplied infinitely every single Planck second. They had been multiplying infinitely, by themselves, recursively, for forty thousand years. The Weakness Paradox evolution had shattered any possible mathematical ceiling millennia ago, transforming his arcane traits into an incomprehensible engine of infinite scaling. The infinitesimal fraction of magic he allowed into the room was so vast, so heavy, so incomprehensibly dense, that it could instantly and effortlessly erase the entire omniverse, and then recreate it, and then erase it again, an infinite number of times, without him ever noticing the expenditure.

The air in the observatory simply ceased to be air. It became a solid block of Godric’s intent.

The Weaver-Choir, these ancient, multi-dimensional hyper-sorcerers of anti-magic, instantly realized the fatal, mind-shattering error of their hubris. The geometric pillars of screaming light abruptly froze. Their attack against my shield evaporated, completely consumed by the ambient, overwhelming weight of Godric’s raw arcane supremacy.

Godric did not use a specific spell. He did not use fire, or lightning, or spatial rending. He simply directed his infinitesimally small fraction of magical offense toward the abstract entities, and he decided they should experience suffering.

The abstract, non-physical entities were violently, aggressively forced to obey his arcane command. Because they lacked physical bodies to experience the pain he desired, Godric’s magic instantly, effortlessly forged physical forms for them. The geometric pillars of light were brutally, agonizingly transmuted into towering masses of raw, exposed, bleeding nerve endings and slick, pulsing muscle tissue. The transition from abstract math to wet, biological reality was an excruciating, tearing process that ripped their consciousness apart.

The newly formed, towering columns of raw meat immediately began to scream. It was a deafening chorus of absolute, unadulterated, biological agony. Blood, thick and dark, began to rapidly pool on the cracked obsidian floor, slicking the soles of my boots.

Godric watched them bleed with a look of mild, detached apathy. "You came into my home," Godric said, his voice flat, completely devoid of the warmth he had shown me a moment prior. "You terrified my nephew. You stained my floor."

With a casual, almost bored flick of his gaze, Godric’s magic ripped the towering masses of exposed nerves apart. He did not unmake them cleanly. He flayed them conceptually. He grabbed the fundamental, arcane strings of their existence and began to slowly, methodically pull. The giant columns of raw meat violently thrashed, spraying hot, arterial blood across the walls and the ceiling, showering me in a thick, metallic mist. They tore at their own newly forged flesh, trying to escape the unbearable, infinitely multiplying friction of his magical grip.

I watched, completely paralyzed, my knees shaking so violently I could barely remain standing. The smell of copper and void-rot was suffocating. I heard the sickening, wet sounds of their biological forms being crushed, twisted, and shredded by an arcane pressure that dwarfed the combined gravity of a billion black holes. Muscle tore from bone that had only existed for a few seconds. Eyes, spontaneously generated across their fleshy surfaces purely to weep tears of agony, violently popped under the strain, leaking vitreous fluid into the growing pool of gore.

Godric did not stop at their physical forms. His magic hunted down the conceptual origin of their existence. The Weaver-Choir had spent millennia hiding their true, core essence in a heavily fortified, hidden dimension outside the bounds of normal reality. Godric’s magic effortlessly bypassed their dimensional locks, shattering their abstract fortresses as easily as a man walking through a cobweb. He grabbed their core essence and dragged it into the observatory, forcing it into the bleeding, shredded meat.

"Please," a collective, gurgling voice wept from the pile of thrashing gore. It was the sound of hyper-sorcerers broken beyond the concept of repair. "Mercy. Oblivion. Please."

Godric did not grant oblivion. He simply squeezed his magical intent. The towering masses of bleeding flesh imploded violently, crushed into a microscopic singularity of pure, concentrated suffering. The singularity hung in the air for a fraction of a second, emitting a high-pitched, whining frequency of eternal torment, before Godric casually flicked it away into the void, banishing it to a pocket dimension where it would burn in that exact state of agonizing compression for the rest of eternity.

The observatory fell completely, sickeningly silent, save for the wet dripping of blood from the walls and the quiet, bubbling groans of the two mutated guards slowly dying on the floor.

Godric let out a soft, measured sigh. He turned his head, his absolute concealment immediately wrapping back around his infinite, world-ending magic, hiding the apocalyptic abyss behind the facade of the ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old king. The warm, kind, friendly smile returned to his face, crinkling the corners of his eyes.

He stepped carefully over a large puddle of steaming blood, his heavy leather boots leaving wet, red prints on the obsidian, and walked over to me. I was trembling uncontrollably, my breath coming in short, ragged gasps. I looked up at him, my mind violently attempting to reconcile the loving, gentle uncle I knew with the absolute, omnipotent, merciless god of magic who had just casually, effortlessly tortured a coalition of abstract entities to death using a fraction of a fraction of his power.

"Are you alright, Gorm?" Godric asked gently, reaching out a warm, calloused hand to firmly grip my shaking shoulder. His touch was incredibly grounding, a solid anchor in a reality that had just permanently lost its structural integrity.

I swallowed hard, the taste of blood and ozone thick on my tongue. "Uncle Godric," I whispered, my voice cracking, barely audible over the roaring existential dread filling my skull. "You... your magic. You... you don't need me. You don't need any of us. My magic... it's nothing."

The cognitive dissonance was a physical blade twisting in my gut. He didn't need protection. He didn't need healing. He didn't need the Court Wizards. His magic was multiplying infinitely every Planck second. He could erase the entire omniverse infinite times over without thinking. He could, I realized with a sudden, horrifying clarity, effortlessly delete the very concept of magic from ever existing in the cosmos. He could unwrite the arcane laws of reality with less effort than it takes to blink. He had not invented magic—Thorin had been a wizard before the stasis event, before Godric became this impossible thing—but Godric could unmake it forever.

Godric’s smile softened into something deeply tender, deeply nostalgic. He looked past me, his gaze seemingly piercing the veil of forty thousand years, looking back to an ancient, forgotten kingdom that no longer existed. He was looking at Thorin. He was looking at Blake.

"I have never needed protection, Gorm," Godric said softly, his thumb gently rubbing my shoulder. "But I need my friends. I need my family. Thorin was a wizard. Blake became a wizard. Your bloodline is tied to the arcane."

He looked back down at me, his eyes filled with a profound, unyielding affection. "I did not create magic, my boy. But I allow it to exist, purely because it is the language you speak. I keep you by my side because I love you, and because seeing you stand in front of me, ready to fight an abstract horror to protect an old man... it reminds me of why I let this omniverse keep spinning."

He patted my shoulder warmly, then turned and began to walk toward the exit, carefully stepping around the mutated guards. "Come along, Gorm. Let us have the servants clean this mess. I believe we have a council meeting to attend."

I stood frozen in the center of the blood-soaked observatory. My pride as the magical shield of Thronegate had been completely, utterly annihilated. My understanding of my own power, my massive, thousandfold daily amplification, had been reduced to a pathetic, microscopic joke. I was a speck of dust claiming it could cast a shadow over a sun. The existential dread was overwhelming, a heavy, suffocating blanket that I knew I would wear for the rest of my life. The gap between us was not just insurmountable; it was a mathematical impossibility.

Yet, as I watched his broad back retreat toward the heavy adamantine doors, I felt a deep, fierce surge of loyalty and love for the man. He was the ultimate apex god predator, a being of such terrifying, absolute supremacy that his slightest whim could permanently rewrite existence. But he was also Uncle Godric. He kept a universe of magic alive simply because he loved his best friend's descendants.

The horror was cemented forever in my soul. I knew, with absolute, unshakeable certainty, that opposing him was the definition of fatal futility. Any entity, any cosmos, any god that dared to stand against him would not just lose; they would be violently, agonizingly unmade in ways that defied sanity. But I was not his enemy. I was his court wizard.

I wiped the violet-tinged blood from my nose with the back of my trembling hand, took a deep, shuddering breath of the copper-scented air, and hurried to follow my king.

"Coming, Uncle Godric," I called out, stepping carefully over the steaming gore. The dread remained, cold and absolute in my chest, a permanent reminder of the impossible, merciless god I served. And I would serve him, faithfully and terrified, until the end of my days.

The heavy, leather-bound tome slammed onto the polished obsidian table with a resonant, final thud that vibrated up through my forearms and settled deep within the marrow of my bones. My father, Caelan, High Court Wizard of Thronegate, let his calloused hand rest upon the worn cover for a long, silent moment. The veins on the back of his hand were a tangled roadmap of violet luminescence, the visible symptom of a thousandfold daily amplification that had been compounding since his twentieth year. He was sixty-two now. By the standards of any other reality, he was a walking cataclysm, a being whose magical capacity could fold galaxies into origami. But tonight, in the dim, flickering glow of the archival chamber's enchanted sconces, he looked impossibly old, impossibly tired, and deeply, profoundly afraid.

"Every Court Wizard before you has read these journals, Kaedan," my father said, his voice a low, gravelly rasp that echoed softly off the towering shelves of ancient scrolls. "My father made me read them when I was your age. His father before him. All the way back to Josh, the son of Blake himself." He slid the tome across the cold obsidian toward me. The cover was unadorned, blank save for a single, embossed symbol: the ancient crest of Olympus, a kingdom that had been ash and forgotten dust for over a million years. "This is mandatory reading. It is the most important lesson you will ever learn as Godric's Court Wizard."

I looked down at the tome, my throat dry. I was twenty-two years old, fresh into my Awakening, the daily thousandfold multiplication of my strength, speed, endurance, and magical capacity still a raw, bleeding wound of overwhelming sensation. My veins throbbed with that same faint, bruised violet light, a constant, physical reminder of the obscene power I was struggling to contain. I was already, by any reasonable cosmic standard, a nascent god. And yet, sitting in this dim room with my father, I felt like a frightened toddler clutching a wooden sword.

"What is it?" I asked, my voice barely a whisper.

Father pulled his hand back, his expression grim. "Witness testimony," he said. "Over the past one million years, countless oppositions have risen against our King. Coalitions. Armadas. Conceptual entities. Hyper-deities. They have tried every conceivable strategy, every impossible weapon, every arcane exploit. All of it has been absolutely, completely useless." He tapped the cover with a heavy finger. "But sometimes, Godric becomes curious. He wonders what would happen if he dismantled an opposition using only one isolated aspect of his amplified stats. No magic. No superpowers. No Gent. No immune response. Just one stat, held back to an infinitesimal, practically nonexistent fraction of its true magnitude."

He turned away, walking toward the heavy iron door. "The Court Wizards who witnessed these incidents were obligated to write detailed, traumatic accounts. They are not historical records. They are warnings. They are lessons in the absolute, mathematical futility of opposing the apex predator of the omniverse." He paused at the threshold, his back still turned. "I was there for the Year 850,000 incident, Kaedan. I wrote one of those entries myself. Read them all. And when you are finished, you will understand why we serve him not just out of love, but out of the absolute, terrifying certainty that defiance is a joke the universe played on the arrogant."

The iron door groaned shut behind him, leaving me alone with the tome.

I opened it. The first page was a simple, handwritten inscription, the ink faded with age but perfectly preserved by powerful stasis enchantments. It read:

*"To all who come after, read and despair. Not for yourselves, but for the foolish who still believe there exists a weapon, a strategy, or a god capable of harming our King. There is no hope. There never was. Serve with love, for the alternative is an eternity of agony."* — Josh, Son of Blake, Year 52.

---

**Excerpt from the Journal of Elias, High Court Wizard, Year 4,500.**

*My hand still trembles as I write this. It has been three days since the Cleansing of the Voraath Horde, and I have not slept. I cannot sleep. Every time I close my eyes, I see the meat. The endless, screaming, liquefied meat.*

*The Voraath were not gods. They were not sorcerers. They were a bio-genetic swarm from the Null-Space between multiverses, a trillion-strong legion of hyper-evolving, adaptive monstrosities. Their flesh was a perfect, biological weapon. They possessed no central nervous system to target, no vital organs to rupture. Every single cell in their bodies was a stem cell, capable of instantly regenerating into any necessary tissue. Sever a limb, and it would sprout a new Voraath warrior from the dismembered flesh. Burn them, and they would evolve heat-resistant carapaces. Freeze them, and their blood would transmute into cryogenic acid. They had consumed over ten thousand universes before arriving at the borders of Thronegate, entirely confident in their adaptive invincibility.*

*Godric met them alone in the void above the capital. He ordered Gent to stand down. He deactivated all passive shields. He suppressed every apocalyptic superpower. He completely severed his connection to the arcane, rendering himself magically inert. He announced, with that warm, casual tone he uses when discussing the weather, that he wished to test his physical strength. Just his strength. Nothing else.*

*When I say he used his strength, I must clarify what that means. Godric's physical strength has been amplifying since the dawn of his stasis one million years ago. At the start, it multiplied by one thousand, and then by itself, every Planck second. After the Weakness Paradox evolution, the multiplication rate transcended infinitely incalculable mathematics. His strength now multiplies by an incomprehensibly massive number every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second, recursively, forever. The gap between his strength at any given instant and his strength one Planck second prior is wider than the gap between an atom and an omniverse.*

*Now, take that infinite, recursive well of impossible force. Godric reduced the amount of strength he would use to an infinitesimal, unfathomably tiny fraction. I am not speaking of one percent. I am not speaking of a decimal followed by a trillion zeros and a one. The fraction he used was so small that if you subtracted it from zero, the concept of mathematics would not register a difference. It was, for all practical and theoretical purposes, zero percent of his total physical strength.*

*Then he clenched his fist.*

*The void did not crack. The void did not tear. The void, and the trillion-strong Voraath Horde occupying it, simply ceased to be a void. The concussive force of his grip was so obscenely, impossibly massive that the surrounding space-time collapsed into a hyper-dense, screaming singularity of pure kinetic pressure. The trillion Voraath warriors did not have time to adapt. Their cells, their legendary hyper-regeneration, their billion-year evolution, meant absolutely nothing. The pressure wave that erupted from Godric's fist slammed into the swarm with such overwhelming, instantaneous force that every single Voraath cell was simultaneously, violently compressed. They did not explode. Explosion implies expansion. They imploded. Every warrior, every drop of adaptive biomass, every screaming maw, was crushed into a microscopic slurry of liquified tissue and pulped chitin. The sound was a wet, deafening, galaxy-spanning CRUNCH that echoed through the fabric of reality for hours afterward.*

*But Godric had misjudged the containment. The Shockwave was supposed to stop at the swarm. Instead, the mere shockwave of his clenched fist—not the strike itself, just the ambient pressure wake—rippled outward. It annihilated seventeen adjacent universes. Not conquered. Not subjugated. Annihilated. The universes were erased from existence as thoroughly as a candle flame snuffed by a hurricane. Trillions of innocent, conquered civilizations, all loyal subjects of Thronegate, were instantly atomized by the side-effect of Godric's "restrained" strength test.*

*I watched the cosmic devastation through the observatory scrying lens, my hands gripping the edges of the console so hard the metal buckled. I felt the distant, cold shudder of seventeen entire realities being erased, and my mind began to crack. But before the fracture could fully form, Godric casually flexed his fingers. And in exactly zero seconds, without a flicker of effort, without a command or a spell, every single one of those seventeen destroyed universes was perfectly, flawlessly restored. Every planet. Every civilization. Every soul. They were all back, their memories unbroken, as if the annihilation had never happened. Because for Godric, it effectively hadn't. The restoration was so casual, so utterly trivial, that he was already walking back toward the palace before the first restored heartbeat had even finished its resumed rhythm.*

*I collapsed to my knees. The existential dread is a permanent, calcified weight in my soul now. I am a man whose magical capacity amplifies a thousandfold daily. I can conjure supernovas with a flick of my wrist. And I know, with absolute, bone-deep certainty, that if Godric ever used even zero-point-zero-zero-zero—I cannot write enough zeros to convey the smallness—percent of his strength against me, I would not just die. I would be retroactively erased from every timeline, every memory, and every possibility, so thoroughly that the omniverse would forget strength itself ever existed.*

*This is not power. This is the death of the concept of opposition.*

I closed my eyes, my breath shuddering out of my lungs in a ragged, uneven gasp. The Voraath Horde. A trillion-strong swarm of hyper-adaptive, universe-consuming monsters. Erased by a clenched fist held back to functionally zero percent of its capability. The collateral annihilation of seventeen universes, restored so casually it didn't even register as an inconvenience.

I turned the page.

---

**Excerpt from the Journal of Tomas, High Court Wizard, Year 38,200.**

*I used to believe that speed was the ultimate equalizer. If you could strike your enemy before they could react, before they could even perceive the threat, then their overwhelming power meant nothing. You could kill a god in the space between heartbeats, if only you were fast enough.*

*The Chronos-Ascendancy believed this too. They were a civilization of temporal purists, beings who had transcended linear time entirely. They existed in the gaps between Planck seconds, moving through reality at velocities that made light seem like a frozen statue. Their warriors could traverse the circumference of a universe in less than a picosecond. Their assassins could kill you in the past before you even formed the intention to threaten them in the present. They were untouchable, undetectable, and utterly arrogant.*

*They declared war on Thronegate by delivering their declaration to the throne room yesterday. By the time we finished reading the parchment, the declaration had already been delivered, read, and rejected in every possible quantum timeline branch simultaneously.*

*Godric seemed almost amused. He had never tested his speed before, he said. Not in isolation. He ordered Gent dormant, suppressed all other attributes, and declared he would meet the Chronos-Ascendancy in the raw, unadorned arena of velocity. He would use nothing but his speed. His speed, he clarified, reduced to an infinitesimal fraction so small that if you divided it by infinity and then divided that result by infinity again, it would still be an overestimation.*

*The Chronos-Ascendancy launched their assault from every possible temporal vector simultaneously. A billion temporal assassins moved to kill Godric in the past. A billion more moved to kill him in the future. A billion more attacked from parallel presents. They coordinated their assault across every Planck second of history, a perfect, inescapable temporal pincer designed to eradicate him from the timeline entirely.*

*Godric simply took a single step forward. Not a run. Not a dash. A casual, relaxed stride, the kind you take when walking from your bedroom to the kitchen for a morning cup of tea.*

*The results were catastrophic. His speed, even reduced to a fraction so incomprehensibly tiny that calling it zero was an insult to the concept of zero, was still an infinite, recursive engine of impossible velocity. The mere act of his foot moving through space at that negligible fraction of his true speed generated a friction wave so intense that the fabric of time itself ignited. The temporal assassins, spread across every moment of history, were violently, simultaneously dragged into a single, compressed instant. Their billion-year temporal assault collapsed into a single Planck second. And in that Planck second, Godric's single, casual step carried him through every single one of them.*

*I witnessed it through the high-speed scrying lenses, slowed down to a billionth of a billionth of a percent of normal perception, and I still could not track him. He did not punch them. He did not kick them. He simply moved through them. The sheer, physical velocity of his body displacing the temporal medium created a cavitation effect of pure, concentrated kinetic agony. The temporal assassins were flayed. Their bodies, simultaneously occupying every possible temporal state, were ripped apart at the subatomic level by the friction of his passage. Their flesh did not tear; it was scoured from existence. Their bones did not break; they were atomized into clouds of screaming quantum foam. The billion-strong temporal armada was reduced to a thin, expanding mist of superheated particulate, their very souls shredded into non-existence by the sheer impossibility of his velocity.*

*But the collateral damage was the true horror. His casual step, that infinitesimally fast stride, did not just kill the Chronos-Ascendancy. The velocity ripple propagated backward and forward through the entire timeline of the omniverse. Every universe, every dimension, every reality, experienced a simultaneous temporal quake. The clocks on a trillion worlds shattered. The chrono-engines of a million space-faring civilizations violently detonated. The flow of time itself stuttered like a skipping phonograph, and for a single, horrifying instant, the entire omniverse experienced a simultaneous moment of frozen, screaming stillness.*

*Godric stopped walking. He looked around at the frozen omniverse, sighed softly, and snapped his fingers. The temporal damage was instantly, perfectly reversed. Every shattered clock reformed. Every detonated engine reassembled. The flow of time resumed its steady march, as if nothing had ever happened. The Chronos-Ascendancy, however, were not restored. Their erasure was not collateral; it was intentional, and it was permanent.*

*I am sitting in my study now, staring at my own hands. My bloodline has served Godric for thirty-eight thousand years. My magic is a terrifying force by any rational metric. And yet, I cannot stop thinking about the speed. He took a step. A single, casual step. And he unmade a civilization that had conquered time itself.*

*What is the point of magic? What is the point of anything? We are motes of dust clinging to the hem of an infinite hurricane.*

I swallowed hard, the copper taste of my own adrenaline flooding my mouth. I had always prided myself on my reflexes, the supernatural quickening of my perception that came with my Awakening. But reading Tomas's words, I understood with sickening clarity that my speed, my thousandfold daily amplification, was a joke. If Godric ever moved with intent against anything in this omniverse, the conflict would end before it even began.

I turned another page.

---

**Excerpt from the Journal of Hadrian, High Court Wizard, Year 120,700.**

*Durability. The word feels meaningless now, a child's scrawl trying to describe a supernova. I have spent the past twelve hours trying to articulate what I witnessed, and every attempt has crumbled into incoherent, babbling horror.*

*The opposition called themselves the Erasure Compact. They were an alliance of omniversal nihilists, beings who worshipped the concept of absolute nothingness. They did not want to conquer Thronegate. They did not want to rule. They wanted to delete existence entirely, to reduce the omniverse to a silent, eternal void. To this end, they constructed an engine of unimaginable destructive potential: a concentrated beam of pure, refined anti-reality, capable of erasing entire multiversal clusters from the fabric of being. It was powered by the willing sacrifice of a trillion nihilist souls, their essences converted into a single, perfectly focused lance of unbeing.*

*They aimed this lance at Godric's heart. They fired. The beam traversed the distance between their dying reality and our throne room in exactly zero seconds, striking Godric's chest with the cumulative force of a trillion self-sacrificing annihilation events.*

*Godric did not dodge. He did not block. He did not summon a shield. He simply stood there, his arms at his sides, his expression one of mild, detached curiosity. He had, he later told me, always wondered what would happen if someone tried to test his durability. So he allowed the strike. He suppressed every other attribute, every passive defense, every reactive aura. He reduced his durability to a fraction of a fraction of a fraction—a value so impossibly, infinitely small that calling it zero-point-zero-zero-zero continued to infinity still vastly overestimated the actual amount he allowed.*

*The lance of absolute erasure struck his chest. It should have unmade him. It should have deleted the very concept of his existence from the omniverse.*

*The beam shattered. Not his chest. The beam. The concentrated force of a trillion apocalyptic sacrifices, a lance of pure anti-reality capable of erasing multiversal clusters, struck his immensely nerfed durability and simply... broke. It splintered into a trillion screaming, reflective shards of deflected annihilation, each shard ricocheting wildly through the dimensional fabric. The Erasure Compact watched in absolute, soul-crushing horror as their ultimate weapon was casually, effortlessly negated by the sheer, passive unyieldingness of his flesh.*

*Then the shards of their own reflected attack hit them. The anti-reality lances, now randomly vectored across the omniverse, began systematically erasing the Erasure Compact's own civilizations. Their fleets. Their star systems. Their entire multiversal cluster was methodically, agonizingly deleted from existence by the deflected shrapnel of their own failed god-killer. They screamed. Oh, how they screamed. The sound of a trillion nihilists being erased by their own stolen fire is a symphony of despair that will echo in my nightmares for the rest of my mortal life.*

*But the collateral damage did not stop with them. The trillions of deflected anti-reality shards continued their chaotic trajectory, punching through dimensional barriers, erasing entire galaxies, annihilating countless innocent, uninvolved universes across the vast span of the omniverse. The scale of the destruction was beyond comprehension. Entire branches of reality simply ceased to exist, wiped clean by the ricochet of Godric's tested durability.*

*When the last scream faded, Godric looked at the devastation, the silent, empty voids where trillions of universes once spun, and he frowned. It was not a frown of guilt. It was a frown of mild inconvenience. He let out a soft, measured breath, and he willed the omniverse to restore. In exactly zero seconds, effortlessly, without moving a muscle, he perfectly rebuilt every erased universe, every shattered galaxy, every deleted civilization. The Erasure Compact remained erased; their memory was preserved only as a cautionary tale.*

*I am writing this from the medical wing. My hands will not stop shaking. The Healers say my neurological pathways are severely frayed from the sheer psychic feedback of witnessing the annihilation and restoration. They say I will recover. They are wrong. I will never recover. The image of that gaunt, handsome, forty-five-year-old man standing in the center of absolute oblivion, his flesh so impossibly durable that the ultimate weapon of unbeing shattered against it like glass against a mountain... it will haunt me until the day I die. I am durable because of my Awakening. My skin can withstand the detonation of a supernova. And I know, with absolute certainty, that the weakest, most pathetic dead skin cell on Godric's knuckle is infinitely more durable than I will ever be.*

Durability. The word tasted like ash in my mouth. I looked down at my own hands, the faint violet luminescence pulsing beneath my skin. My Awakening had made me tough. I could survive the vacuum of space, the pressure of a black hole. And yet, compared to Godric, I was less durable than a soap bubble in a hurricane.

I turned another page, my heart hammering so hard I could feel it pulsing in my temples.

---

**Excerpt from the Journal of Alaric, High Court Wizard, Year 500,000.**

*I have always considered myself a perceptive man. My Awakening, the thousandfold daily amplification of my senses past the age of twenty, has given me the ability to perceive the entire electromagnetic spectrum, to hear the gravitational hum of distant galaxies, to smell the unique chemical signature of a single atom. I can see through solid matter, perceive the flow of time itself, and detect the faintest whisper of a thought forming in a mind across the room. I believed, naively and arrogantly, that I was a master of sensory information.*

*The Omni-Shroud were a coalition of perfect stealth assassins. They were not physical beings; they were sentient, predatory voids, living absences that consumed all sensory data that touched them. Light fell into them and never escaped. Sound was swallowed whole. Thermal radiation vanished. Psychic probes returned absolute, empty silence. They were the ultimate assassins, beings who could stand directly in front of you, screaming at the top of their nonexistent lungs, and you would perceive absolutely nothing. They had slaughtered countless gods and cosmic entities simply because no one could detect their presence.*

*They came for Godric with the confidence of the undetectable. They surrounded him in the throne room while he sat reviewing agricultural reports, their void-forms pressing in from every angle, their blades of concentrated nothingness poised to strike.*

*Godric did not look up from his reports. He did not even pause his reading. He simply decided to test his senses. He suppressed every other attribute, every passive aura, every reactive defense. He reduced his sensory perception to a fraction so small, so infinitesimally negligible, that if you took the concept of zero and divided it by every infinite number that had ever existed, the result would still vastly overstate the amount he allowed.*

*Then, without a word, he perceived them. I do not mean he saw them, or heard them, or detected their psychic void. I mean he perceived them. The infinitesimal, practically nonexistent sliver of his sensory capability effortlessly pierced their perfect stealth as if it were a pane of clear glass. He did not just detect their location. He perceived their entire history. He smelled the exact atomic composition of their first kill, billions of years ago. He heard the silent death-screams of every victim they had ever consumed, echoing in the void of their forms. He saw the exact moment of their creation, the precise mathematical configuration of their anti-sensory fields, and the pathetic, terrified realization dawning in their nonexistent minds that they had been seen.*

*The Omni-Shroud assassins froze. I could not perceive them, of course. But I could perceive the sudden, overwhelming terror that radiated from their empty forms, a psychic scream of pure, undiluted dread that even their perfect stealth could not contain.*

*"Interesting," Godric murmured, still not looking up from his papers. "You are voids that consume perception. How clever."*

*Then he simply continued to perceive them. He did not attack. He did not command their destruction. He just allowed his infinitesimal, practically-zero-percent sensory perception to rest upon them with its full, passive weight. And the Omni-Shroud, the perfect stealth assassins who could not be detected by gods or cosmic entities, were driven utterly, catastrophically mad. Their sentient voids, which existed solely to consume sensory data, were force-fed an infinite, recursive torrent of perception. Godric's gaze, even at a negligible fraction of its true power, flooded their empty forms with so much raw sensory information that their consciousnesses shattered. They saw themselves. They saw every possible version of themselves across every timeline. They saw the infinite, recursive reflection of their own terrified voids, mirrored endlessly in the depths of Godric's perception.*

*They did not die. They did not explode. They simply... unraveled. Their void-forms, unable to process the infinite sensory overload, voluntarily collapsed into non-existence. One by one, the perfect assassins chose to erase themselves from reality entirely, their final, unified psychic scream a desperate, grateful plea for the sweet release of oblivion.*

*The entire confrontation took less than three seconds. Godric never once lifted his eyes from the agricultural report. He simply finished reading the paragraph about grain yields, made a small notation in the margin with his quill, and moved on to the next page. The Omni-Shroud were gone, erased by their own volition, driven to suicide by the mere passive perception of a man whose senses were held back to effectively zero percent of their true magnitude.*

*I am the High Court Wizard of Thronegate. I can perceive galaxies dying a billion light-years away. I can taste the chemical composition of a comet's tail. And I am, in the face of Godric's perception, completely and utterly blind. He sees everything. He has always seen everything. The only reason any secret has ever existed in this omniverse is because he found the act of keeping it mildly amusing.*

I closed my eyes, my stomach churning. The Omni-Shroud. I had read about them in historical texts—a boogeyman of the cosmic shadows, the ultimate assassins that even the most powerful gods feared. Godric had driven them to suicide without even looking at them.

I turned another page, my hands now visibly trembling.

---

**Excerpt from the Journal of Caelan, High Court Wizard, Year 850,000.**

*My son, Kaedan. If you are reading this, know that your father loves you. Know that the Awakening you carry, the thousandfold daily amplification of your strength and speed and magic, is a gift of impossible generosity. And know that it is, in the face of our King, utterly meaningless. I do not say this to be cruel. I say this because it is the fundamental truth of our existence, and you must understand it if you are to serve without succumbing to the madness of pride.*

*The Luck-Sunderers were not warriors. They were not gods. They were a civilization of probability manipulators, beings who had mastered the fundamental mathematics of chance itself. They could bend causality to their will, ensuring that every sword swung at them missed, every spell fizzled, every bullet jammed, every heart attack struck their enemies at precisely the right moment. They had never lost a conflict because the very concept of losing had been mathematically deleted from their probability matrix. Luck, they believed, was the ultimate weapon. It did not matter how strong or fast or durable an enemy was if the dice of reality always rolled in your favor.*

*They declared their intention to dethrone Godric by broadcasting a probability wave across the entire omniverse, a wave designed to recalculate all mathematical odds in their favor. The wave would, in theory, ensure that Godric experienced the most statistically improbable string of misfortunes in the history of existence. A stray heart attack. A sudden, fatal aneurysm. A quantum fluctuation that erased his physical form. A random, completely accidental collapse of the local space-time that trapped him in a dying pocket dimension. They did not need to overpower him. They just needed luck to favor them.*

*Godric received their declaration with a placid, almost bored expression. He had never tested his luck in isolation before, he mused. It seemed like a worthwhile experiment. He suppressed every other attribute. Every passive defense. Every reactive aura. He reduced his luck to a fraction so small, so unutterably tiny, that if you wrote a decimal followed by every zero that had ever existed in every mathematical equation across the entire omniverse, it would still be an incomprehensibly vast overestimation.*

*The Luck-Sunderers activated their probability wave. The mathematics of chance across the entire omniverse were violently, forcefully recalculated to ensure Godric's absolute, inevitable defeat. Every possible outcome now pointed to his immediate, catastrophic misfortune.*

*And then, nothing happened. No heart attack. No aneurysm. No quantum fluctuation. No space-time collapse. The probability wave, the ultimate weapon of the Luck-Sunderers, simply... failed. It did not fizzle. It did not backfire. It just failed to produce any effect whatsoever. Because even at an infinitesimally small, functionally nonexistent fraction of his true luck, Godric's probability manipulation outweighed theirs by an infinity of infinities. His passive, nerfed luck was so overwhelmingly, impossibly vast that the Luck-Sunderers' probability wave could not even register his existence as a variable. Their calculations simply ignored him, as if he were a blind spot in the mathematics of reality itself.*

*But the horror did not end there. The probability wave, unable to affect Godric, instinctively sought out the next most valid target. It turned on its creators. The recalculated odds, desperate to fulfill their programming, violently inverted. Every single Luck-Sunderer, across every dimension of their civilization, simultaneously experienced the most statistically improbable string of fatal misfortunes ever conceived. Their hearts all stopped at the exact same nanosecond. Their brains all suffered catastrophic, simultaneously fatal aneurysms. Their dimensional fortresses all coincidentally drifted into the paths of random, wandering quantum singularities. Their backup consciousnesses, stored across a billion secure servers, all simultaneously, accidentally corrupted. Their entire civilization, a trillion-strong empire of probability masters, was wiped out in less than a second by the sheer, passive recoil of their own luck manipulation failing to account for an untouchable variable.*

*And the collateral damage? The probability wave, ricocheting wildly after erasing its creators, continued to randomly alter the odds across the entire omniverse. Stars went supernova in regions that had been stable for billions of years. Peaceful civilizations suddenly experienced catastrophic, statistically impossible natural disasters. The fundamental luck of trillions of innocent beings was violently, chaotically scrambled. The omniverse descended into a brief, horrifying maelstrom of probability chaos.*

*Godric watched the chaos unfold for approximately three seconds. Then, with that same casual, effortless finality, he willed the omniverse to restore. In exactly zero seconds, every scrambled probability was corrected. Every supernova was reversed. Every disaster was undone. The Luck-Sunderers remained erased, a permanent monument to the futility of opposing his luck.*

*I have served Godric for eight hundred and fifty thousand years, Kaedan. I love him. I revere him. And I am more terrified of him than I am of the void itself. He is not lucky. He is the fundamental source code from which the very concept of luck derives its meaning. The dice of the omniverse have been loaded in his favor since the first Planck second of creation, and they will remain loaded forever. Remember this when you take my place. There is no probability, no chance, no statistical anomaly that can save his enemies. They are doomed the moment they form the thought of opposition, and the only thing their struggle does is feed his already infinite supremacy.*

*I love you, my son. Serve well. And never forget what you read here.*

I set the journal down on the cold obsidian table, my breath coming in short, ragged gasps. My father. The man who had raised me, who had taught me my first spells, who had tucked me into bed with stories of ancient heroes and noble kings. He had witnessed an entire civilization erased by the passive recoil of their own weapon, their luck rendered obsolete by a fraction of Godric's luck so small it functionally did not exist. And he still stood beside the throne every single day, his love for his King an unshakable, terrified constant.

I turned another page, my fingers numb.

---

**Excerpt from the Journal of Valerius, High Court Wizard, Year 999,999.**

*I am an old man, or at least I feel like one. My Awakening keeps my body strong, my magic sharp, but my soul is weary. I have served Godric for nearly two hundred thousand years, and I have witnessed horrors that would shatter the minds of lesser men. But nothing, absolutely nothing, prepared me for the Charisma Incident.*

*They called themselves the Unbreakable Convocation. They were an alliance of a million civilizations, a coalition so vast and so ideologically unified that their combined will was a physical, tangible force. They did not attack with weapons or magic. They attacked with rhetoric, with ideology, with the sheer, overwhelming force of their absolute, unshakable conviction. They broadcast their message across the entire omniverse: a perfect, mathematically irrefutable argument for Godric's tyranny, a philosophical virus designed to turn his own subjects against him, to make him doubt his own divinity, to unravel his empire from within by the sheer force of persuasive truth.*

*Their leaders were a cadre of hyper-charismatic demigods, beings whose voices could sway galaxies, whose presence could convert entire civilizations to any cause with a single, passionate speech. They had united a million warring species under a single banner of rebellion, not through conquest, but through the sheer, overwhelming power of their charisma. They did not need to defeat Godric in combat. They would defeat him in the hearts and minds of his own people.*

*Godric invited them to the throne room. I stood at his right hand, my heart hammering against my ribs. The leaders of the Convocation entered, a procession of radiant, impossibly magnetic beings, their charisma rolling off them in waves that made even me, a loyal servant of four hundred thousand years, feel a flicker of doubt about my King. They began to speak, their arguments a symphony of pure, undeniable logic, their presence a suffocating, beautiful weight that seemed to bend reality itself toward their cause.*

*Godric listened. He let them speak for nearly an hour. He nodded along at their finer points. He seemed, to my mounting horror, genuinely engaged with their rhetoric. Then, when they had finished their grand, soaring peroration, he smiled. It was a warm, friendly smile, the smile of a man greeting an old friend. And then he spoke a single word.*

*"No."*

*He had, he later explained, decided to test his charisma. He suppressed every other attribute, every passive aura, every reactive defense. He reduced his charisma to a fraction of a fraction—a value so infinitesimally small, so utterly negligible, that if you took the entirety of time since the dawn of the omniverse and divided it by his charisma, you would still have an infinite number of zeros before you reached any meaningful digit.*

*And yet, that single word, spoken with that infinitesimally tiny fraction of his charisma, ended the war. The leaders of the Unbreakable Convocation, the hyper-charismatic demigods who had united a million civilizations with their words, instantly, completely, and irrevocably abandoned their cause. They did not just surrender. They fell to their knees, weeping tears of absolute, shattering adoration. They worshiped him. They begged, with desperate, choking sobs, for his forgiveness. The force of his charisma, even at that infinitely negligible exposure, had completely overwritten their convictions, their ideologies, their entire sense of self. They became his most zealous, fanatical devotees in the span of a single syllable.*

*But the collateral damage was the true nightmare. The charisma wave, rippling outward from that single, softly spoken word, propagated across the entire omniverse. Every single subject of the million rebelling civilizations, every single soldier in their vast armadas, every single citizen on their countless worlds, simultaneously experienced the exact same, overwhelming conversion. The rebellion did not just end. It inverted. The combined military might of a million civilizations instantly became Godric's most fiercely loyal army. Their generals immediately turned their weapons on any remaining holdouts, slaughtering their former comrades with the fanatical zeal of the newly converted.*

*The bloodshed was catastrophic, beyond comprehension. The new converts, desperate to prove their devotion, engaged in an orgy of self-cleansing violence that massacred countless trillions across the omniverse. They tore their own civilizations apart in their frenzied need to demonstrate their love for their new, singular, absolute King. The screaming, the begging, the rivers of blood—I watched it all through the scrying lenses, my hands glued to my sides in frozen, helpless horror.*

*Godric watched for a long, silent moment. Then he sighed, a soft, measured sound of mild inconvenience. And in exactly zero seconds, without a single gesture or command, he restored every innocent who had been slaughtered in the frenzy. Every civilian. Every conscript who had been forced into service. Every child. He restored them all, their memories of the horror wiped clean, their lives resumed as if the massacre had never occurred. The original leaders of the Convocation remained converted, their worship eternal and unbreakable, a permanent, living monument to the sheer, terrifying magnetism of his voice.*

*I am writing this with a steady hand, but my soul is not steady. My soul will never be steady again. I am a man whose charisma is magnified a thousandfold daily. I can sway nations with a glance. And I know, with absolute, soul-crushing certainty, that if Godric ever spoke my name with even that infinitesimally tiny fraction of his charisma, I would tear out my own heart and offer it to him on a silver platter, weeping tears of joy as I died. Charisma is not a gift. It is a leash, and every neck in the omniverse wears his collar.*

I closed the tome. The heavy leather cover fell shut with a resonant, final thud that echoed through the dim archival chamber like a death knell. I sat there in the oppressive, enchanted silence, my hands flat against the worn cover, my breath shallow and ragged, my veins pulsing with that faint, violet luminescence that suddenly felt less like a gift and more like a brand.

One million years. One million years of recorded, traumatic witness testimony, each entry a monument to the absolute, mathematical impossibility of opposing Godric. Strength that erased universes with a clenched fist. Speed that ignited the fabric of time with a casual stride. Durability that shattered the ultimate weapon of unbeing without a scratch. Senses that drove perfect assassins to suicide with a passive glance. Luck that inverted reality's probabilities against its own masters. Charisma that converted a million rebelling civilizations with a single, softly spoken word.

And every single one of those impossible, catastrophic demonstrations had been performed with a fraction of Godric's true power so infinitesimally, infinitely small that calling it zero percent was a grotesque, laughable overestimation. His stats had been multiplying, infinitely and recursively, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second, for over a million years. The Godric who had clenched his fist in Year 4,500 was infinitely, incomprehensibly weaker than the Godric who now sat on the throne above me. And the Godric who would exist one Planck second from now would be infinitely, incomprehensibly stronger than the Godric of this moment. The math was a descending spiral into absolute, crushing madness.

I understood, now, why the reading was mandatory. It was not a history lesson. It was not a warning. It was an inoculation against pride. Every Court Wizard of my bloodline, from Josh to my father, had read these journals and had their arrogance surgically, traumatically excised. We were not protectors. We were not shields. We were beloved pets, cherished companions whose magic was kept relevant only because our King loved us and found our presence comforting.

I stood from the obsidian table on legs that felt hollow and unsteady, the tome clutched against my chest. The violet luminescence in my veins throbbed with a steady, rhythmic pulse, a constant reminder of the thousandfold daily amplification that made me a walking god by any rational metric. And yet, as I climbed the winding staircase back toward the throne room, I felt smaller than I had ever felt in my life. A speck of dust, clinging to the hem of an infinite, recursive hurricane.

The throne room doors loomed before me, massive slabs of adamantine carved with the ancient crest of Olympus. I pushed them open. Godric was standing by the arched window, his back to me, his heavily muscled frame silhouetted against the golden light of the setting Thronegate sun. He was exactly as the journals had described him: a forty-five-year-old man, stunningly rugged and handsome, his short salt-and-pepper hair neatly combed, his beard perfectly trimmed. He radiated the warm, simple presence of a protective, friendly father. There was absolutely no aura, no pressure, no hint of the infinite, recursive, soul-crushing godhood that lay hidden behind the flawless veil of his absolute concealment.

He turned as I entered, and his face broke into a warm, genuine smile that crinkled the corners of his kind eyes. "Kaedan," he said, his voice a rich, comforting baritone that vibrated perfectly in my chest. "You have finished the reading."

It was not a question. Absolute omniscience. He already knew every page I had turned, every shuddering breath I had taken, every terrified realization that had bloomed in my mind.

"Yes, Your Majesty," I said, my voice barely a whisper. I walked forward, my legs still trembling, and took my place at his right hand. The spot where my father had stood for centuries. The spot where Valerius, Hadrian, Tomas, Elias, and every other Court Wizard of my bloodline had stood before me.

Godric reached out and placed a warm, calloused hand on my shoulder. The touch was grounding, solid, impossibly reassuring despite the cold, calcified dread that now permanently occupied the base of my skull. I looked up into his eyes, those kind, fatherly eyes, and I saw nothing but genuine affection. He was not a tyrant wearing a mask of warmth. He was a warm, loving king who also happened to be an infinite, recursive engine of absolute, merciless annihilation to anyone who dared oppose him.

"I am glad you are here, my boy," Godric said softly, his thumb gently rubbing my shoulder. "Your father speaks very highly of you. And I see so much of Blake in your eyes."

Blake. The legendary best friend. The mortal merchant who had stumbled into a forgotten sanctuary and accidentally released the apex predator of creation from his stasis, a million years ago. The man who had anchored Godric's buried warmth, who had been the catalyst for the kind, just king who now stood before me.

I swallowed hard, the taste of copper and existential dread thick on my tongue. "I will serve you with my life, Your Majesty," I said. "I have read the journals. I understand the futility of opposition. I understand what you are."

Godric's smile softened, and there was a flicker of something ancient and sad in his eyes. "Do you, Kaedan?" he asked quietly. "Do you truly understand?"

I thought of the Voraath Horde, erased by a clenched fist. The Chronos-Ascendancy, flayed by a casual stride. The Erasure Compact, shattered against his passive durability. The Omni-Shroud, driven to suicide by his gaze. The Luck-Sunderers, inverted by their own probability wave. The Unbreakable Convocation, converted by a single word. And I understood, with sudden, terrifying clarity, that I did not understand. I could not understand. The gap between his existence and mine was not a measurable quantity. It was a fundamental, unbridgeable chasm that no amount of Awakening amplification could ever hope to cross.

"No," I admitted, my voice cracking. "I do not understand. But I do not need to. I only need to serve. And to love you, as my father loves you. As all my ancestors loved you."

Godric's smile widened, the warmth returning full force to his eyes. "That is all I have ever asked for," he said. "That is all I have ever wanted." He squeezed my shoulder gently, then turned back toward the window, his gaze sweeping out over the glittering, infinite spires of his capital, the heart of an empire that spanned the entire omniverse.

"Welcome to your post, Kaedan," he said. "Welcome to the family."

I stood at his right hand, my veins throbbing with violet light, my soul anchored by a cold, heavy weight of absolute, existential dread. And I loved him. I loved him with the desperate, terrified love of a man who understood that his existence was a gift, permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex god predator who could erase the omniverse with a stray, idle thought. The horror was permanent now, a foundational pillar of my consciousness. But so was the love. So was the loyalty.

My father had been right. The journals were not warnings. They were lessons in the absolute, mathematical futility of opposing Godric. And I had learned the lesson well. I would serve him, faithfully and terrified, until the end of my mortal days. Because the alternative, the alternative that every enemy in the journals had suffered, was an eternity of agonizing, inescapable, conceptually unfair annihilation. And I, like every Court Wizard before me, had no desire to become another entry in the tome of traumatic witness testimony.

I was Kaedan, High Court Wizard of Thronegate, descendant of Blake, servant of the omnipotent, omniscient, infinitely amplifying apex god of the omniverse. And I would never, ever forget the horror that slept behind my King's kind, fatherly smile.

# Godric's Stats Journal Example

**Year 12,000 — The Incident of Absolute Speed**
**Penned by Arch-Mage Vane, 8th Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The handwriting in this journal was precise, academic, and utterly devoid of emotion, the script of a man who had dissociated so completely from the horror he had witnessed that his pen could only record the clinical facts. The ink was a faded, rusty brown, and the parchment crackled like frozen leaves as I turned the pages.

*I am writing this account three hours after the conclusion of the Chronal Incursion. My hands are not trembling, though I suspect this is only because my nervous system has entered a state of profound, protective shock from which it may never fully emerge. I must record what I witnessed before the memory degrades, though I suspect the memory will outlast my physical body and persist, screaming, into whatever afterlife awaits the servants of the apex predator.*

*The Chrono-Reapers were not a species in any conventional sense. They were hyper-dimensional assassins who had evolved beyond linear biology to become living weapons of temporal erasure. Their bodies were composed of solidified negative time, and they moved backward through the causal stream with a fluidity that made the concept of "speed" meaningless. They did not dodge attacks. They simply ensured that the attacks had never been launched, sliding backward to the moment before the hostile intent was formed and erasing the attacker from the timeline with surgical precision. They had unmade seventeen thousand civilizations in this manner, each one erased so completely that not even the memory of their existence remained in the cosmic record.*

*When Thronegate's envoys arrived to deliver the offer of unconditional surrender, the Chrono-Reapers did not negotiate. They simply reached backward through the timeline and erased the envoys' grandparents from ever having been born. Our diplomats flickered out of existence, their entire bloodlines retroactively annihilated, leaving behind only a faint, aching void in the memories of those who had known them.*

*Godric received the report in the throne room. He was sitting perfectly motionless upon the obsidian dais, His ruggedly handsome face illuminated by the amber glow of the arcane sconces. His short salt-and-pepper hair did not stir. His heavily muscled frame, draped in simple dark garments, remained absolutely still. He required no breath, no sustenance, no sleep. He was an immortal monolith of apocalyptic power, and He had been sitting in that exact position for three days, simply existing, because existence was all He needed to do.*

*I delivered the report myself, my voice steady despite the cold dread coiling in my gut. Godric listened without expression. His absolute omniscience had already perceived the erasure of the envoys, had already traced the temporal manipulation backward to its source, had already catalogued every atom of the Chrono-Reapers' existence. He knew everything. He simply nodded once and rose from the throne.*

*He materialized in the Chrono-Reapers' temporal nexus, a vast, churning maelstrom of inverted causality that existed outside the boundaries of linear time. The Reapers, numbering in the conceptual decillions, surrounded Him instantly, their negative-time bodies flickering across every possible moment of attack simultaneously. Their High Executor, a towering vortex of solidified temporal negation, broadcast a declaration directly into the fabric of reality. It declared that the Chrono-Reapers could not be outsped, outmaneuvered, or outlasted. They existed at every point in time simultaneously, and they would erase Godric from the timeline before His first heartbeat had ever occurred.*

*Godric did not activate His magic. He did not summon Gent or deploy any of His infinite passive shields. He withheld exactly ninety-nine point nine-nine percent of His true power, continuing the nines infinitely into a recursion of self-negation so profound that the value of His restraint could not be mathematically expressed. He wanted to test a single stat. His Speed.*

It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. *He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the fourth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty billion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty billion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across thousands of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Trillions of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a millisecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the thousands of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*

The heavy, iron-banded door to the archival vault slammed shut behind my father with a sound like a sepulchral tomb sealing itself against the living world. The enchanted sconces upon the towering obsidian shelves flickered, their flames guttering low for a moment before steadying into a cold, unwavering blue luminescence that cast jagged, skeletal shadows across the ancient stone floor. I stood motionless in the center of the vast, silent chamber, my hands hanging limp at my sides, my veins throbbing with a faint, bruised violet luminescence that pulsed in rhythm with my quickening heart. The Awakening had ignited within my flesh exactly seven hundred and thirty days ago, when I crossed the threshold of my twentieth year, and every single day since that morning my physical strength, my kinetic speed, my magical capacity, and every other measurable attribute of my being had multiplied by a factor of one thousand. I was twenty-two years old, the newest and youngest Court Wizard in the million-year history of the Thronegate Empire, and by any rational metric known to the civilizations that cowered beneath our sovereign's shadow, I was a nascent god of staggering, terrifying power.

And yet, as I stared at the single, heavy tome resting upon the cold obsidian lectern in the center of the vault, I felt smaller than a single, dying atom drifting through the infinite void.

My father, Caelan, had not spoken a single unnecessary word during the long, silent descent into the archival depths. His craggy, weathered face, framed by the same thick mutton chops that had adorned every portrait of Blake's descendants for a thousand millennia, had been set in an expression of profound, bone-deep exhaustion. He had simply placed the tome upon the lectern, laid his calloused, violet-veined hand upon its cracked leather cover, and looked at me with eyes that held the cold, bottomless weight of absolute, unutterable dread.

"Every Court Wizard before you has read these journals, Kaedan," he had said, his voice a low, gravelly rasp that scraped against the oppressive silence like a blade against whetstone. "My father made me read them when I was your age. And his father before him. All the way back to Josh, the son of Blake, who penned the first entry in Year 52." He had paused, his thumb tracing the embossed crest of the forgotten kingdom of Olympus that adorned the cover. "They are not histories. They are not lessons. They are the recorded, traumatic witness testimony of every Court Wizard who has watched our King dismantle an opposition with a single, isolated fraction of His power. You will read every word. You will understand the absolute, mathematical futility of opposing the apex predator of the omniverse. And when you are finished, you will join me at His right hand and serve Him with the same terrified, unwavering love that has bound our bloodline to His throne for a million years."

Then he had turned and walked out, and the door had slammed shut, and I was alone with the tome.

I approached the lectern on legs that felt hollow and unsteady, my amplified heart hammering a frantic, terrified rhythm against the cage of my ribs. The tome was massive, easily a thousand pages of faded, brittle parchment bound between covers of cracked, ancient dragon-hide. I opened it to the first entry, my fingers trembling, and began to read.

---

**Year 6,000 — The Incident of Absolute Senses**
**Penned by Vaelin, 3rd Court Wizard of Thronegate**

The handwriting was cramped, angular, and deeply erratic, the ink a faded, rusty brown that had bled into the parchment fibers like a spreading bruise. The script was the work of a man whose mind had fractured under the weight of what he had witnessed and who had only managed to reassemble the shattered pieces long enough to record the horror before his sanity failed him entirely.

*I am writing this account from the deepest sub-chamber of the medical wing. My ears have not stopped bleeding. The Healers inform me that my auditory nerves are severely frayed, that I will likely suffer a permanent, ten-percent reduction in my sensory acuity for the remainder of my mortal life. I consider this a profound mercy. I would gladly sacrifice ninety-nine percent of my hearing if it meant I could forget the Sound that He made.*

*The Silent Choir were not a species in any conventional framework of biological classification. They were sentient, predatory vibrations that existed in the negative spectrum of audible reality, beings composed of pure, weaponized Anti-Sound. They did not communicate through conventional phonation; they emitted frequencies of absolute auditory negation that erased sound from existence, creating expanding voids of perfect, fatal silence that suffocated the eardrums, the auditory cortex, and ultimately the very consciousness of their victims. A universe that fell within their expanding radius of silence simply went deaf. Every voice. Every heartbeat. Every whisper of wind and roar of solar combustion. Erased. The inhabitants would claw at their own ears, screaming sounds they could no longer hear, until the silence grew so absolute that their minds, deprived of all auditory stimulus, simply collapsed into catatonic, eternal hysteria. The Silent Choir had devoured three hundred thousand inhabited galaxies in this manner before they reached the outermost perimeter of Thronegate's sphere of subjugation.*

*They did not negotiate. When our envoys approached with the standard offer of unconditional surrender, the Choir simply emitted a single, sustained frequency of Anti-Sound that retroactively erased the envoys' voices from ever having existed. Our diplomats returned to the capital physically intact but completely, permanently mute, their vocal cords reduced to smooth, non-functional muscle tissue, their minds shattered by the sudden, absolute loss of all capacity for speech.*
